sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 56-year-old male, presents with a 4-5 week history of difficulty swallowing, describing a sensation of something being stuck in his throat, which is more pronounced with solid foods.
He reports occasional pain and the need to wash down solid food with liquids to facilitate swallowing.
He denies coughing or choking, but has occasionally thought about potentially choking.
He does not notice any temperature-related modifications to his swallowing difficulty.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
He has been on metformin 500mg twice a day for his diabetes and norvasc for his hypertension.
His hemoglobin A1c was last checked at 7.4, and he reports adhering to his medication regimen and improving his diet and exercise.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any blood in his stool, vomiting, or coughing up blood.
He reports no weight loss or changes in his weight.
He also denies chest pain or shortness of breath.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes; Norvasc (dosage not specified) for hypertension.
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: 120/50
Temperature: Not mentioned
Pulse rate: Not mentioned
Oxygen saturation: Not mentioned
Physical exam:
The patient's neck is fine, with no swelling or masses.
The thyroid is normal, and there are no lymph nodes.
The throat examination is normal, with no masses or swelling.
The lung exam is clear, and the heart exam is normal with no murmurs.
The patient has epigastric tenderness on the right side, but no masses or significant swelling.
The back exam is normal, and the extremity exam is fine.
The neurological exam is normal.
Test Results:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has difficulty swallowing (dysphagia), which may be due to a narrowing of the esophagus (stricture).
The patient also has diabetes and hypertension.
Problem list:
Dysphagia; Diabetes; Hypertension
Plan:
I recommended a referral to gastroenterology for further evaluation and potential endoscopy to investigate the cause of the patient's difficulty swallowing.
I also prescribed Prilosec 20mg once a day for possible gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD).
I instructed the patient to chew their food thoroughly and to return for follow-up in a month.
I also ordered a repeat hemoglobin A1c and kidney function tests.
Additionally, I referred the patient to ophthalmology for a diabetic eye examination.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient in a month to review the results of the blood tests and make any necessary adjustments to their medication regimen.
I will also refill the patient's norvasc prescription.
